items.q: Why do we always see the same side of the Moon?
items.correct: It rotates once for every orbit of Earth (synchronous rotation).
items.misconception: The Moon does not spin at all.
items.random_wrong: Earth's shadow hides the far side.
items.why: The Moon does spin - its rotation simply matches its orbit, keeping one face toward us.

items.q: Is there a permanently dark side of the Moon?
items.correct: No - the far side receives just as much sunlight; it is only hidden from Earth.
items.misconception: The far side is always in darkness.
items.random_wrong: The Moon makes its own light.
items.why: 'Far side' means unseen from Earth, not unlit; it goes through day and night like the near side.

items.q: How did the Moon's craters form?
items.correct: From impacts by meteoroids and asteroids.
items.random_wrong: From the Moon shrinking and wrinkling.
items.why: Most lunar craters are impact scars, preserved because the Moon has no weather to erode them.

items.q: The dark patches on the Moon (maria) were formed by:
items.why: It's Ancient volcanic lava flows. “Oceans of water” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Why does the same side of the Moon always face Earth?
items.correct: It rotates once per orbit (synchronous rotation)
items.misconception: It does not rotate at all
items.why: Correct answer: It rotates once per orbit (synchronous rotation). “It does not rotate at all” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The Moon's surface is covered by a layer of loose dust and rock called:
items.why: Correct answer: Regolith. “Magma” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Craters survive on the Moon for billions of years because it has:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Almost no atmosphere or weathering. “Strong winds” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly how many days does the Moon take to orbit the Earth once (sidereal, rounded)?
items.why: The real figure is 27 days. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
